coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots confined in N-doped carbon as peroxidase-like nanozyme for detection of gastric cancer relevant D-amino acids
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Zhe
Liu, Wendong
Ni, Pengjuan
Zhang, Chenghui
Wang, Bo
Duan, Guangbin
Chen, Chuanxia
Jiang, Yuanyuan
Lu, Yizhong
coredata.dc:description:
The concentrations of D-Proline (D-Pro) and D-Alanine (D-Ala) in the saliva of early-stage gastric cancer patients are prominently higher than those of healthy people. Therefore, precisely monitoring the concentrations of D-Pro and D-Ala has essential implications for gastric cancer diagnosis and treatment. Here, through pyrolysis of ZIF-8 precursor filled with glucose (G@ZIF-8), the carbon dots confined in N-doped carbon (CDs@NC) are successfully obtained. The CDs@NC can readily act as peroxidase-like nanozyme for the colorimetric detection of D-amino acids that associated with early gastric cancer. Intriguingly, the peroxidase-like activity of CDs@NC can be tuned easily by adjusting the content of glucose precursors. Further study of the catalytic mechanism shows that the superoxide radical (O2
−) may be the critical Reactive oxygen species (ROS) involved in the peroxidase-like catalytic reactions. The excellent results of saliva tests suggest that the colorimetric method could be used to quickly distinguish patients with early gastric cancer from healthy people.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Confinement effect
Peroxidase-like
D-amino acids
Gastric cancer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Steady-state kinetic assay of the CDs@NC-3
Synthesis and characterization of CDs@NC nanozymes.
Enzyme mimicking activities of CDs@NC nanozymes

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21904048 , 21902062 and 21902061 ), the Young Taishan Scholar Program ( tsqn201812080 ) and the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province ( ZR2019YQ10 and ZR2020QB033 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The synthesis mechanism diagram of CDs@NC-3 by two-step calcination method with ZIF-8 as a template. (b–e) SEM images and (f–i) TEM images of ZIF-8, G@ZIF-8, CDs@ZIF-8, CDs@NC-3, respectively. (j) TEM image and (k) HRTEM image of CDs@NC-3. The dots in the red circle are carbon dots. Inset in Fig. 1k shows the lattice fringes of CDs in CDs@NC-3.

(a) XRD patterns of ZIF-8, G@ZIF-8, CDs@ZIF-8, glucose (G), G-200 and CDs@NC-3. (b) XRD patterns and (c) FTIR spectra of CDs@NC-1, CDs@NC-2, CDs@NC-3 respectively. (d) N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms and (e) pore-size distributions of ZIF-8, NC and CDs@NC-3. (f) XPS survey scan of CDs@NC-3. (g) O 1s, (h) C 1s and (i) N 1s spectra of CDs@NC-3.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of different reaction systems using TMB as substrate. Insets: corresponding digital pictures. (b) Absorbance in relation to time at 652 nm for CDs@NC-1, CDs@NC-2, CDs@NC-3 respectively. (c) Comparison of peroxidase activities of different samples, all the samples’ concentrations are 0.5 mg mL−1. Insets: corresponding digital pictures. (d) Comparison of different peroxidase activity. (e) The absorbance of the CDs@NC-3 system with different concentrations scavengers. (f) The ESR spectra of the DMPO-CDs@NC-3 methanol solution. Each error bar in (b), (d) and (e) represents the standard deviation of three independent measurements.

Steady-state kinetics assays of CDs@NC-3 by varying concentrations of (a) TMB and (c) H2O2 and (b, d) corresponding Lineweaver-Burk plots. Each error bar represents the standard deviation of three independent measurements.

(a) Schematic diagram of detection mechanism of D-Pro and D-Ala by using CDs@NC-3. The UV–vis absorption spectra of various reaction systems about (b) D-Pro and (e) D-Ala. Absorption spectra of reaction systems containing different concentrations of (c) D-Pro and (f) D-Ala. Insets: corresponding digital pictures. Relationship between the concentrations of (d) D-Pro and (g) D-Ala and absorbance at 652 nm. Insets: calibration curves for D-Pro and D-Ala detection. Each error bar represents the standard deviation of three independent measurements.

(a) Detection of the possible interferences in the saliva. All the samples’ concentrations are 1 mM. (b) The absorbances at 652 nm of the solution when different proportions of D-amino acids are present. All the samples’ concentrations are 1 mM. Each error bar represents the standard deviation of three independent measurements. (c) Test of 10 clinical saliva samples (5 times diluted). Each error bar represents the standard deviation of five independent measurements. (d) Results of tests on clinical samples presented in tabular form.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots confined in N-doped carbon as peroxidase-like nanozyme for detection of gastric cancer relevant D-amino acids

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
The concentrations of D-Proline (D-Pro) and D-Alanine (D-Ala) in the saliva of early-stage gastric cancer patients are prominently higher than those of healthy people. Therefore, precisely monitoring the concentrations of D-Pro and D-Ala has essential implications for gastric cancer diagnosis and treatment. Here, through pyrolysis of ZIF-8 precursor filled with glucose (G@ZIF-8), the carbon dots confined in N-doped carbon (CDs@NC) are successfully obtained. The CDs@NC can readily act as peroxidase-like nanozyme for the colorimetric detection of D-amino acids that associated with early gastric cancer. Intriguingly, the peroxidase-like activity of CDs@NC can be tuned easily by adjusting the content of glucose precursors. Further study of the catalytic mechanism shows that the superoxide radical (O2
−) may be the critical Reactive oxygen species (ROS) involved in the peroxidase-like catalytic reactions. The excellent results of saliva tests suggest that the colorimetric method could be used to quickly distinguish patients with early gastric cancer from healthy people.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to the fast pace of life and poor eating habits, the incidence and mortality of gastric cancer are the highest among cancer-related deaths in the world [1–5]. Researches have shown that the 5-year overall survival rate after advanced treatment for gastric cancer is between 10% and 30%, but the overall survival rate for early treatment is 92.6% [3]. Therefore, early detection and timely treatment are of great significance [6–8]. However, most patients with early gastric cancer have no specific symptoms, making it a real hassle for prompting diagnosis and effective treatment [3]. Traditional diagnostic methods for gastric cancer include blood test, biopsy and endoscopy [9]. Nevertheless, all of these methods are complicated, time-consuming, depend on rigorous laboratory facilities and may even adverse to human tissues [3,8]. Therefore, there is an urgent need for convenient and rapid methods of diagnosing early gastric cancer, especially those based on analyzing easily and non-invasively obtained clinical samples.

It has been reported that salivary D-amino acid concentration has a striking correlation with early gastric cancer [3,10]. Concretely, the concentrations of D-proline (D-Pro) and D-alanine (D-Ala) in the saliva of early gastric cancer patients are 126.3–285.3 μM and 46.1–114.5 μM, respectively, significantly higher than those of healthy people (7.3–18.3 μM and 5.4–7.8 μM, respectively) [3,10,11]. Thus, precisely monitoring the concentrations of D-Pro and D-Ala has essential implications for gastric cancer diagnosis and treatment. At present, several detection methods have been reported, including chromatography [12]., electrochemiluminescence [13]., and electrochemical biosensors [14]. However, the colorimetric method for detecting D-Ala and D-Pro is rarely reported in spite of its simpler implementation. Previous studies have demonstrated that D-amino acid oxidase (DAAO) can selectively oxidize D-Ala and D-Pro to produce H2O2 [3,15,16]. Therefore, coupling enzyme labels with some substrates-H2O2 system, colorimetric platform is expected to achieve the colorimetric detection of D-Pro and D-Ala.

As a combination of enzyme-like catalyst and nanomaterial, nanozymes have aroused considerable research interest in the fields of analytical sensing and biomedicine in the past few years [17–26]. Among them, Carbon dots (CDs) as enzyme mimics have attracted much attention for their low cost, good biocompatibility and nontoxicity [27–31]. However, a tendency to agglomerate, the non-uniform size and morphology will badly degrade the enzyme-like activity of CDs [32,33]. Encapsulation with suitable template can efficaciously overcome these predicaments. In this viewpoint, Zeolitic imidazolate framework (ZIF-8), a representative metal-organic framework material, is an excellent candidate due to its stable skeleton and uniform internal pore size (~1.9 nm) [33]. The skeleton of ZIF-8 can prevent the accumulation of various nanoparticles and makes their size and morphology uniform [33–35]. In particular, carbonizing ZIF-8 at high temperature can get N-doped carbon, which can further improve the catalytic activity of peroxidase mimics [24,36]. However, the confinement effect of CDs in N-doped carbon to enhance peroxidase activity has not been reported before.

Here carbon dots confined in N-doped carbon (CDs@NC) is successfully fabricated via two-step carbonization of the ZIF-8 filled with glucose (G@ZIF-8). The G@ZIF-8 precursor is prepared through a traditional chemical doping method, in which glucose molecules can unimpededly infiltrate into the ZIF-8 cavity. After pyrolysis at 200 °C for 2 h, uniform-sized CDs with high dispersity confined in ZIF-8 (CDs@ZIF-8) are obtained, and then CDs@NC are formed by the further carbonization at 900 °C for 3 h. The CDs@NC possesses impressive peroxidase-mimicking activity, which can be easily tuned through regulation of glucose content. The confinement of the CDs guest inside the N-doped carbon host plays a pivotal role in the improvement of catalytic activity. The CDs@NC is used successfully for colorimetric detection of D-Pro and D-Ala, and the final clinical test results are well consistent with our expectations. This nanozyme-based colorimetric assay makes the detection of gastric cancer simpler and quicker.
Chemicals and instrumentation can be found in the Supporting information.

ZIF-8 rhombic dodecahedron is synthesized according to the experimental method previously reported [37]. Firstly, 1.68 g Zn(NO3)2·6H2O dissolved in 20 mL of methanol (MeOH) was added into 60 mL of MeOH solution that containing 4 g 2-Methylimidazole (2-MeIM). After stirring vigorously at room temperature for 1 h, the mixture was then left to rest at room temperature for 24 h. The product was isolated by centrifugation and washed three times with MeOH. Finally, ZIF-8 powder was obtained after overnight vacuum drying at 60 °C.

Typically, 200 mg powder of ZIF-8 was immersed into different concentrations of Glucose (G) solution (1 mM, 2 mM, 3 mM, 4 mM, 5 mM, VEtOH/Vwater = 9:1) respectively, then stir vigorously at room temperature for 6 h. The obtained products were separated by centrifugation and washed three times with mixed solution (VEtOH/Vwater = 9:1), and finally dried in a vacuum at 60 °C for 24 h.

The powder of G@ZIF-8 that spread evenly in the porcelain combustion boat was carefully placed in a tube furnace, and then was heated from room temperature to 200 °C for 3 h with a heating rate of 5 °C min−1 under nitrogen-flow. After cooling to room temperature, the CDs@ZIF-8 was obtained.

The powder of CDs@ZIF-8 was transferred into a tube furnace and heated from room temperature to 900 °C for 3 h with a heating rate of 5 °C min−1 under nitrogen-flow. The resulting powders were labeled as CDs@NC-1, CDs@NC-2, CDs@NC-3, CDs@NC-4 and CDs@NC-5 according to the different glucose concentrations. For comparison, we also synthesized several other materials (CDs, NC and CDs + NC). CDs are obtained by etching CDs@ZIF-8 with 1 mM KOH solution. NC are obtained by heating ZIF-8 at 200 °C nitrogen atmosphere for 3 h and then heating at 900 °C nitrogen atmosphere for another 3 h. CDs and NC with a mass ratio of 1:1 were dissolved in deionized water, the mixture was mechanically stirred at room temperature for 6 h, then centrifugally washed and dried to obtained CDs + NC.

By using 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) as a substrate, the peroxidase-like activity of samples was measured. Normally, 20 μL CDs@NC-x samples (x = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 0.5 mg mL−1) were mixed with 830 μL acetate buffer (pH 4.0, 0.2 M), 50 μL TMB substrate (5 mM) and 100 μL H2O2 (0.1 M). After being incubated at 25 °C for 20 min, the absorption spectra of the mixed solutions were recorded by the UV–vis spectrophotometer.

Typically, 730 μL of acetate buffer (pH 4.0, 0.2 M), 50 μL of TMB substrate (5 mM), 100 μL of H2O2 (0.1 M), 20 μL of CDs@NC-3 (0.5 mg mL−1) and 100 μL of scavengers (tryptophan, isopropanol or p-benzoquinone) with different concentrations were mixed thoroughly, and the absorption spectra were recorded after being incubated at 25 °C for 20 min. By using the 5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) as spin trapping agent, the generation of the superoxide anion (O2
−) radicals was further confirmed by the electron spin resonance (ESR) experiment. In a typical measurement of O2
−, 200 μL of CDs@NC-3 methanol solution (0.5 mg mL−1) and 1 mL of H2O2 (0.1 M) were added into 7.8 mL of 0.2 M acetate buffer (pH 4.0). Immediately, 1 mL of DMPO (500 mM) was added into the above mixture solution. The resulting solution was extracted by quartz capillary tube and placed in a glass tube for the ESR detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Steady-state kinetic assay of the CDs@NC-3
ce:section.ce:para:
Steady-state kinetics experiment was operated at room temperature. Typically, 830 μL of acetate buffer (pH 4.0, 0.2 M), 50 μL of TMB (0–1 mM), 20 μL CDs@NC-3 (0.5 mg mL−1) and 100 μL of H2O2 (0.1 M) were mixed, followed by monitoring the reaction-time curves. Similarly, in order to obtain the kinetic data for H2O2, 830 μL of acetate buffer (pH 4.0, 0.2 M), 20 of μL CDs@NC-3 (0.5 mg mL−1), 50 μL of TMB (5 mM) and 100 μL H2O2 (0–100 mM) were mixed. By fitting the reaction velocity values and the substrate concentrations to the Michaelis-Menten equation, the kinetics constants (i.e., V max and K m) were calculated as follows:
V =
V max
[S]
K m
+[S]
where V is the initial reaction velocity, Km is the Michaelis constant, [S] is the substrate concentration, and Vmax is maximal reaction velocity.

For the detection of H2O2, 830 µL of acetate buffer (pH 4.0, 0.2 M), 20 μL of CDs@NC-3 (0.5 mg mL−1), 50 μL of TMB substrate (5 mM) and 100 µL of H2O2 with different concentrations were mixed and incubated at 25 °C for 10 min, then the absorption spectra of the mixed solutions were monitored.

For the detection of D-Pro, 200 μL of PBS (phosphate buffer saline) buffer (pH 6.8, 0.2 M) containing 10 μL of DAAO (10 U mL−1) and D-Pro with various concentrations were incubated at 37 °C for 45 min. Then, 730 μL of acetate buffer solution (pH 4.0, 0.2 M), 20 μL of CDs@NC-3 (0.5 mg mL−1) and 50 μL of TMB (5 mM) solutions were added into the above solution. After reaction at 25 °C for another 30 min, the UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded. The detection of D-Ala was the same as the above method, just with the D-Ala replaced D-Pro.

For the detection of clinical saliva samples, 200 μL of clinical saliva samples and 10 μL of DAAO (10 U mL−1) were incubated at 37 °C for 45 min. Then, 720 μL of acetate buffer solution (pH 4.0, 0.2 M), 20 μL of CDs@NC-3 (0.5 mg mL-1) and 50 μL of TMB solutions (5 mM) were added into the above solution. After reaction at 25 °C for another 30 min, the UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CDs@NC nanozymes.
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1 a, CDs@NC nanozymes were prepared by two-step calcination of G@ZIF-8. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) and Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images show the as-prepared ZIF-8 nanocrystals are featured with a typical rhombohedral dodecahedron structure and uniform particle size of about 250 nm (Fig. 1b and f). ZIF-8 nanocrystals contain abundant internal pores and provide the possibility for the loading of glucose, an economical carbon source that can be readily carbonized to CDs at 200 °C [33,38]. Due to the relatively smaller size, glucose can be imbedded into cavity of ZIF-8 host (denoted as G@ZIF-8) without changing the morphology of pristine ZIF-8 (Fig. 1c and g) [33]. After the pyrolysis at 200 °C in a nitrogen atmosphere for 3 h (denoted as CDs@ZIF-8), there seems to be no change in the sample's morphology at the first glance (Fig. 1d and h). In fact, the interior structure of the G@ZIF-8 after pyrolysis has changed dramatically. As is represented in Fig. S1, the separated CDs with a uniform size of about 2 nm can be released from the cavity of ZIF-8 by immersing the CDs@ZIF-8 in 1.0 mM KOH solution, in which the ZIF-8 host is thoroughly etched. The above results indicate that the framework of ZIF-8 retains its initial dodecahedral shape after the first pyrolysis at 200 °C, while the guest glucose molecules are transformed into CDs which are confined into the cavity of ZIF-8. We then heated the CDs@ZIF-8 in nitrogen flow at 900 °C for another 3 h. The framework of the ZIF-8 is completely carbonized and evolved into porous N-doped carbon structures, while the CDs are uniformly confined in the carbon frameworks (denoted as CDs@NC). HRTEM image of CDs@NC-3 shows some dot-like particles with the size of about 2 nm, manifesting the existence of CDs in the porous carbon structure (Fig. 1k). The lattice spacing of CDs is 0.21 nm (inset of Fig. 1k), which is the same as the graphene (100) plane and consistent with previous relevant literature reports [39,40]. Additionally, we also investigate the different samples (CDs@NC-1, CDs@NC-2, CDs@NC-3) obtained by adding different concentrations of glucose (1, 2 and 3 represent the addition of 1 mM, 2 mM and 3 mM glucose, respectively). As shown in Fig. S2, as the amount of glucose increases, the particles size of CDs increases. For comparison, N-doped carbon (NC) is also prepared by pyrolyzing non-glucose filled ZIF-8 at 200 °C nitrogen atmosphere for 3 h and then heating at 900 ℃ nitrogen atmosphere for 3 h (Fig. S3).

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns shown in Fig. 2 a demonstrate the diffraction peaks G@ZIF-8 are in accordance with that of the ZIF-8, suggesting the load of glucose molecules has a negligible effect on the crystal structure of ZIF-8, which is coincided with the TEM results above. After being heated at 200 °C for 3 h, glucoses are carbonized to CDs, which can be revealed by the XRD patterns of G and G-200. Whereas, CDs@ZIF-8 shows semblable diffraction characteristic to ZIF-8 due to the low dosage of CDs and the robustness of ZIF-8 framework at 200 ℃. As shown in Fig. 2b, the XRD patterns show that the CDs@NC samples with different content of glucose precursors have two characteristic peaks at 25° and 43° corresponding to the (002) and (100) planes of graphite carbon, respectively [41,42], indicating that CDs@ZIF-8 is completely carbonized. The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) testing shows that there are abundant functional groups on the surface of CDs@NC. As shown in Fig. 2c, there is a strong absorption peak corresponding to O–H at 3432 cm−1, and the peak at 1384 cm−1 is ascribed to N–H, the presence of –OH and –NH groups increase the solubility of CDs@NC. The strong absorption peak located at 1656 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibration region of the –CO groups, which act as the catalytically active sites for converting H2O2 to Reactive oxygen species (ROS) [43–45]. As shown in Fig. 2d and Fig. 2e, compared with ZIF-8, the pore size of NC has no significant change, while the pore size of CDs@NC-3 is significantly reduced, demonstrating that a substantial fraction of pores inside the NC are filled with CDs. The elemental compositions of the CDs@NC-3 can be determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses. C, N, and O are found in the CDs@NC-3 from the XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 2f). It has been reported that carbonyl groups play crucial roles in determining the peroxidase-like activity of carbon-based nanozymes. More specifically, the OC–O– groups serve as the binding sites for H2O2, and –CO groups act as the catalytically active sites for converting H2O2 to ROS [43,45]. For CD@NC, both –CO groups and OC–O– groups can be found (Fig. 2g and Fig. 2h,), which will enhance the catalytic efficiency of CD@NC. The N 1s spectra (Fig. 2i) shows four strong characteristic peaks located at 403.5 eV, 398.7 eV, 400.6 eV and 398.0 eV, which correspond to oxidized N, pyrrolic N, graphitic N, and pyridinic N, respectively [46,47]. Graphitic N and pyridinic N are the two favorable nitrogen species for catalytic reactions. Graphitic N is advantageous to improve the adsorption of catalytic substrate, and pyridinic N is helpful for capturing H2O2 and protons [24,48]. Therefore, O, C and N elements act a significant part in whole catalytic reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enzyme mimicking activities of CDs@NC nanozymes
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 3 a, CDs@NC-3 delivers an excellent peroxidase-like activity. With the presence of H2O2, CDs@NC-3 can catalytically oxidize colorless TMB to blue color oxidized TMB (oxTMB) with a characteristic peak centered at 652 nm. To explore the effect of glucose concentration on the catalytic performance of CDs@NC, the peroxidase-like activities of CDs@NC-x (x = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5) are compared (Fig. 3b, c and Fig. S4). Obviously, the catalytic activity of CDs@NC-x enhances gradually with increasing glucose concentration from 1 to 3 mM, and the catalytic activities of CDs@NC-4 and CDs@NC-5 are not significantly enhanced compared with CDs@NC-3. The key factor of glucose concentration to improve catalytic performance may be the number of generated CDs that confined in NC. As the amount of glucose increases, the amount of glucose molecules entering the pore size of ZIF-8 also increases, resulting in more confined CDs to favor the nanozymatic performance of CDs@NC. When the amount of glucose molecules further increases, the internal pores of ZIF-8 are saturated, so the catalytic activity will not be significantly improved. The CDs@NC-3 are chosen for the following study because of their best peroxidase-like activity. To explore the role of the carbon host and CDs guest in the catalytic activity of CDs@NC-3, we have synthesized NC host and CDs separately and investigated their catalytic activities. As shown in Fig. 3c and d, both NC and CD exhibit peroxidase-like activity, but their activities are far inferior to that of CDs@NC-3. It indicates that the catalytic activity of CDs@NC-3 is extremely enhanced when CDs guest is in-situ confined in the carbon host. In stark contrast, the activity of CDs + NC after mechanical mixing is not significantly enhanced, indicating synergetic effect is not the dominate factor in the improvement of catalytic activity. So, we think that it is the confinement effect that plays a pivotal role in the improvement of catalytic activity [49–51]. Like natural peroxidase, the peroxidase-like catalytic activity of CDs@NC-3 is rely on the concentrations of catalyst (Fig. S5a) and H2O2 (Fig. S5b), pH (Fig. S6a) and temperature (Fig. S6b). As the concentrations of H2O2 and TMB increase, the activity of CDs@NC-3 also increases. The activity of CDs@NC-3 changes with the alteration of pH and reaches the maximum at pH = 4, which is similar to the natural peroxidase. CDs@NC-3 exhibits high-activity in the normal temperature range (20–40 °C) and this provides excellent convenience for clinical application.(Fig. 3.)

The catalytic intermediates are studied to explore the catalytic mechanism of CDs@NC-3 (Fig. 3e and Fig. S7). Tryptophan, p-benzoquinone and isopropanol are used as the scavengers for singlet oxygen (1O2), O2
− and hydroxyl radicals (
OH), respectively [52–54]. As shown in Fig. S7a and b, isopropanol and tryptophan have negligible effects on the CDs@NC-3-catalyzed TMB oxidizing reaction, manifesting that
OH and 1O2 are not involved in the catalytic process. However, the increase in p-benzoquinone can obviously inhibit the catalytic reaction (Fig. S7c), indicating that O2
− plays dominant role in the catalytic reaction of TMB oxidation. The ESR measurements are also conducted to prove the above conclusion. The ESR test of DMPO/O2
− reaction system shows four distinct characteristic peaks with a peak intensity ratio of 1:1:1:1, further manifesting the generation of O2
− (Fig. 3f). The V max and K m of CDs@NC-3 nanozymes are obtained by performing steady-state kinetics assays (Fig. 4 ). Compared to several carbon nanozymes previously reported, CDs@NC-3 has smaller K m and larger V max toward TMB and H2O2 (Table S1), which demonstrates the CDs@NC-3 has a good affinity for substrates (i.e. TMB and H2O2) and a relatively fast reaction rate.

Fig. 5 a represents the detection mechanism diagram. CDs@NC-3, as peroxidase mimic, can degrade weak oxidant H2O2 to produce O2
− with strong oxidizing property, thus oxidizing colorless TMB to blue oxTMB. As H2O2 can be generated from the DAAO-catalyzed hydrolysis of DAA, a sensing platform for DAA can be anticipated. First, the feasibility of this assay is investigated. Fig. S8a exhibits the absorption spectra of reaction systems containing different concentrations of H2O2 range from 0 to 100 mM. Obviously, the absorbance enhances gradually with increasing the H2O2 concentration, accompanied by a progressive deepening in color. The linear detection range is between 10 to 400 μM, and the detection limit is 0.72 μM (Fig. S8b). The detection limit is calculated by 3 N/S, where N represents the standard deviation of blank sample, and S represents the slope of linear range. The successful detection of H2O2 lays the foundation for the subsequent assay of D-Pro and D-Ala. As shown in Fig. 5b and e, single DAA (1 mM) or DAAO (100 U/L) has minor influence on response of the CDs@NC-3/TMB system. However, the A652 markedly increases when 1 mM DAA and 100 U/L DAAO coexisted in the as-designed CDs@NC-3/TMB sensing platform. It is because catalytic hydrolysis of DAA by DAAO produces H2O2 that promotes the oxidation of TMB. Thus, utilizing the peroxidase-mimetic activity of CDs@NC-3, the detection platform of D-Pro or D-Ala can be established.

Fig. 5c and f deliver typical dose-response curve for D-Pro and D-Ala detection with the different concentration range from 0 to 1 mM, respectively. With the increased concentrations of DAA, gradual increases in absorbance and deepening in color can be observed due to the increased concentration of H2O2 in situ produced. A wide linear range of 20 to 300 μM for D-Pro and 20 to 400 μM for D-Ala as well as low detection limits of 7.7 μM for D-Pro and 18.6 μM for D-Ala are obtained, respectively (Fig. 5d and g). Although the sensitivity is lower than some previously reported methods (Table S2), this colorimetric detection system is more convenient and simpler without resorting to complicated operation and expensive instruments. Particularly, the color variation for D-Ala or D-Pro response can be easily distinguished by visual observation (Fig. 5c and f). When the concentrations of D-Pro and D-Ala exceed 10 μM and 20 μM respectively, a visible color change can be distinguished from the original ones.(Fig. 5).

The detection of clinical saliva samples is more complex than that in the laboratory environment. The Cysteine (Cys), Glutathione (GSH), L-proline (L-Pro), L-alanine (L-Ala), glycine, valine, serine, threonine, KCl, MgCl2, CaCl2, sodium carboxymethyl cellulose (SCC), KHCO3, and Methyl-p-hydroxybenzoate (MPHB) in saliva samples may interfere the detection results of D-Pro and D-Ala and then impair the accuracy of gastric cancer diagnosis [3,55]. Thus, we do selective testing for these possible interferents. Fortunately, those other molecules cannot cause the same color change of the solution, confirming the good specificity for DAA (Fig. 6 a). As the proposed assay is based on H2O2-triggered oxidation of TMB with the assistance of CD@NC, only the molecules that generate H2O2 can be recognized and detected. DAAO can chiral specifically catalyze DAAs (alkaline and neutral) to generate H2O2, and no H2O2 can be generated for L-amino acids and other molecules. Therefore, the system can selectively sense the tested D-amino acids, but not other molecules, with the assistance of DAAO. To explore whether the specific detection of one amino acid can be realized when two amino acids are present in the mixture, we test the mixture of D-Pro and D-Ala in different proportions (the total amount of D-Pro and D-Ala is the same). As shown in Fig. 6b, these solutions have almost the same absorbance, indicating the specific detection of one amino acid cannot be achieved.

To explore the possibility of clinical application, we test ten sets of clinical samples from five healthy people in our lab and five patients with gastric cancer from the No. 960th Hospital of PLA (Tai 'an Hospital Area). In the ten samples tested in our detecting system, the saliva of the five healthy people (No. 1–5) show no color response. On the contrary, five patients’ saliva (No. 6–10) show marked discoloration (Fig. 6c). We calculate the total concentration of two D-amino acids in the saliva of ten sets of clinical samples (Fig. 6d). The value of No. (7, 9, 10) samples are slightly higher than the range for patients with early-gastric cancer (174.2 μM–399.8 μM). This may be because patients with gastric cancer who have been diagnosed are essentially advanced gastric cancer. The results show that the system has a good potential on the distinguish of gastric cancer at the early stage.(Fig. 6).

In conclusion, utilizing ZIF-8 as a template, glucose as the filling molecule, CDs@NC nanozymes with high peroxidase mimicking activity are obtained and successfully used to diagnosis early gastric cancer. By varying the concentration of glucose molecules, the peroxidase-like activity can be easily modulated. The free radical scavenging experiment and ESR test indicated that O2
– plays a vital role in the process of reaction. Based on the H2O2-related catalytic activity of CDs@NC, colorimetric measurement of D-Pro and D-Ala is anticipated and achieved. The detection of D-Pro and D-Ala in clinical saliva samples with satisfactory specificity implies its enormous potential to diagnose gastric cancer. Notably, it is possible to detect early gastric cancer only through observing the color change of solution with naked eyes, and the clinical samples can be obtained non-invasively. This makes the diagnosis of early gastric cancer analysis simpler and quicker. This work not only provides an efficacious experimental idea for the synthesis of specific and highly active peroxidase-like nanozymes but also provided a possible practical method for the timely diagnosis of gastric cancer.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21904048, 21902062 and 21902061), the Young Taishan Scholar Program (tsqn201812080) and the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2019YQ10 and ZR2020QB033).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2021.131396.

ce:section.ce:para:
